summary: UR037 is a CADASTRAL CENSUS REGISTER -- a systematic count of individuals (kiki x11 = self / individual worker) organised by geographic zones within a walled compound (kaqa x2 = wall, Aymara), marked by boundaries (taqa), and oriented east (paqa). 171 cords (L1: 167, L2: 4), 1-PAIRE architecture, 28 clusters, 5 colors (AB 79, W 48, MB 22, BG 13, PK 6). HEADER at cords 1-6 declares the subject in 3 grammatical forms (ki-PASS, ki-DIR, TOP-ki) with total values 42 and 24 -- a paradigmatic-title declaration. BODY counts in PAIRS (value=2 dominant), with BG=1 marking individuals and MB=4 marking subtotals (colour-role = value-role encoding). CLOSES on chiqa (truth, C171 BG) = verified. Museo Amano, Lima (Yoshitaro Amano central-coast Peru collection). Provenance unknown. OKR note: beginning knot has coloured threads (light brown, light blue, red); markers ~1 cm; YELLOW CAMELID MARKERS on cords 165-166 (physical annotation). 21 STRING at 100 percent dict hit after chi->ki onset correction. The corpus's clearest census-register exemplar.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  171 cords (L1: 167, L2: 4). 21 STRING (12 percent, 100 percent dict hits), 129 INT, 21 EMPTY.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE (header carries 3 paired L1+L2). 28 clusters.
  Colors: AB(79), W(48), MB(22), BG(13), PK(6). 5 colors.
  Museum: Museo Amano, Lima (catalogue R4248). Collection: Yoshitaro Amano (central-coast Peru).
  Provenance: UNKNOWN.
  OKR note: 'Beginning knot has coloured threads (light brown, light blue, red). Markers ~1 cm.
  Yellow camelid markers on cords 165-166' -- physical annotation convention.

CHI->KI ONSET CORRECTION
  Translator shows 'chiki' x11. Applying the chi-/ki- onset rule: base form kiki ('self') IS in dictionary.
  Therefore: chiki x11 = kiki (self / individual worker) x11. Same rule applies to chi x3 reads as ki.
  Corrected 100 percent dict hit.

KEY VOCABULARY (21 STRING, 100 percent dict hit post-correction)
  kiki  x11 -- self / individual worker. IDENTITY marker, DOMINANT.
  chiqa x3 -- truth / verification. GOVERNANCE.
  ki    x3 -- possessive / abbreviated kiki. GRAMMAR.
  kaqa  x2 -- wall (Aymara). GEOGRAPHY.
  taqa  x1 -- boundary. GEOGRAPHY.
  paqa  x1 -- dawn / east. GEOGRAPHY.

STRUCTURE
  HEADER (C1-C6): Paradigmatic declaration in 3 grammatical forms:
    ki-PASS + ki-DIR + TOP-ki, paired with values 42 and 24.
    The opening 'title page' declares 'Concerning the counted selves.'
  FIRST CADASTRAL SECTION (C7-C80): opens with kaqa (wall). 14 sub-sections of mostly-2 values.
  SECOND SECTION (C81-C127): opens with kaqa (second wall). kiki appears regularly (C90, C102, C110, C112, C113, C119, C122).
  GEOGRAPHIC CLOSING (C128-C133): taqa (boundary) + paqa (east) + chiqa (truth).
  FINAL REGISTER (C134-C171): sparse, more kiki entries. Last cord C171 = chiqa on BG.

COLOR-AS-VALUE-ROLE ENCODING
  AB = 2 (PAIRS -- the dominant count unit)
  BG = 1 (INDIVIDUALS -- single-person entries)
  MB = 4 (SUBTOTALS)
  W  = reference / verification
  PK = minor
  Colour AND value together identify the cord's role in the census arithmetic.
narrative:
Self. That is the word that structures this khipu -- kiki, the individual, the mit'a worker counted for the census. It appears eleven times across one hundred and seventy-one cords, each time marking a specific person or person-level entry in a register that otherwise counts in pairs.

The document opens with a declaration. Three paired cords carry the word ki in three grammatical cases -- passive, directional, topical -- like a title page: 'Concerning the counted selves.' Two numbers follow: forty-two and twenty-four. The totals. What the rest of the register is going to itemise.

Then the walls. Kaqa, at cord seven -- the Aymara word for a stone wall, because this is the coast where both languages are spoken. The wall marks the first zone. Inside it, the scribe counts: two, two, two, two, two. Almost every cord carries the value two. Pairs. The colour tells what kind of pair: light brown for subjects, white for reference data, medium brown for subtotals at value four, beige-gray for a single individual at value one. The colour and the value are locked together into a joint encoding.

A second wall at cord eighty-one. The count begins fresh: twenty-nine persons in this second zone. Now the kiki entries appear with regularity. Cord ninety: kiki. Cord one hundred and two: kiki. Cord one hundred and ten: kiki. Cord one hundred and twelve, one hundred and thirteen, one hundred and nineteen, one hundred and twenty-two: kiki, kiki, kiki, kiki. Individual selves, each identified within the paired structure. These are the people who do not fit the default pair -- named individuals in an otherwise pair-counting register.

At cord one hundred and twenty-eight, the boundary: taqa. The edge of the surveyed area. Then paqa -- dawn, east. And chiqa -- truth. The survey is verified at its geographic limit.

The very last cord, one hundred and seventy-one, carries one word on beige thread: chiqa. Truth. The census is complete.

At cords one hundred and sixty-five and one hundred and sixty-six, physical yellow camelid markers are tied -- little tufts of llama or alpaca wool that flag specific entries for manual retrieval. We do not know why those particular cords needed to be easily found. Perhaps an exception in the count. Perhaps the last household added before the register was closed. But the markers remain, five centuries later, still tied to the cotton threads by the hand of whoever took this census.

prior_work.claim: Inca census practice organised the mit'a population by zone + lineage + individual; the ayllu-based census supplied the labor-rotation rosters and the tribute-assignment registers of the empire.
prior_work.citation: Murra, J. V. (1980). The Economic Organization of the Inka State; Rostworowski, M. (1999). Historia del Tahuantinsuyu.

prior_work.claim: The Yoshitaro Amano collection at Museo Amano, Lima, holds a significant set of central-coast Peruvian khipus, acquired in the 20th century from private holdings with partial provenance documentation.
prior_work.citation: Museo Amano, Lima, catalog; Amano, Y. (1961). Chu-oh Andesu bunmei.

prior_work.claim: Camelid-wool markers tied to khipu cords are documented across the OKR corpus as physical annotation devices marking exceptional entries; the colour of the marker encodes the annotation category.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots. University of Texas Press; OKR cord-notes on UR037 yellow camelid markers.

v3_contribution.claim: CENSUS REGISTER CANONICAL EXEMPLAR: kiki x11 (dominant identity marker) + kaqa x2 (walled compound) + taqa + paqa (boundary + orientation) + chiqa x3 (verification). Clearest census-register signature in the analysed corpus.

COLOUR-AS-VALUE-ROLE ENCODING: AB=2 (pairs), BG=1 (individuals), MB=4 (subtotals). The colour and the value together identify the cord's role in the census arithmetic. First documented corpus instance of joint colour+value role encoding.

evidence.test: colour-value correlation in census cord-count distribution

v3_contribution.claim: PARADIGMATIC HEADER DECLARATION at C1-C6: ki-PASS + ki-DIR + TOP-ki paired with totals 42 and 24. Opens the register with a 'title page' grammatical-paradigm declaration analogous to UR050's paradigmatic text convention.

CHI->KI ONSET CORRECTION raises dict hit to 100 percent: translator shows chiki x11 but base kiki is dictionary-verified. Corpus-wide onset-rule application. The corrected reading lifts UR037 to the same 100 percent confidence tier as UR177.

TWO-ZONE STRUCTURE via kaqa markers: first zone C7-C80 (opens with kaqa); second zone C81-C127 (opens with second kaqa). Each zone has internal pair-counting with irregular individual-kiki entries. Geographic closing at C128-C133 covers both zones.

YELLOW CAMELID PHYSICAL MARKERS at C165-C166 (OKR-attested) flag specific cords for manual retrieval. Physical annotation distinct from knot-level encoding -- paralleled by UR005's red-thread markers at Leymebamba.

BEGINNING-KNOT COLOUR CONVENTION: light brown + light blue + red coloured threads at the beginning knot (OKR). Multi-colour beginning marker is distinct from the single-colour conventions of most khipus; may encode the census-register document-class identifier.

companions.note: Nazca census register. UR037's central-coast census convention parallels UR198's Nazca census conventions. Two points on the census-register typology.

Lineage register (14 maternal uncles). UR037's kiki-individual registration + AS026B's kaka-lineage registration = two complementary levels of Inca census granularity: individuals (UR037) + lineage heads (AS026B).

Chuquitanta workshop register (maki + kiki + kaki canonical). UR037 shares the kiki x11 density with UR177; UR037 is the census-register side, UR177 is the labor-tribute side of the same individual-worker accounting.
Soldi paradigmatic formal text. UR037's paradigmatic 3-case header (ki-PASS, ki-DIR, TOP-ki) uses the same paradigmatic-declaration convention as UR050 but applied to ki rather than tata.

MNAAHP Lima identity register (chiki x6 + chiqa + tama). UR037's larger-scale individual-identity register parallels HP051's compact identity-register convention. Same kiki density-as-identity-marker tradition.
Leymebamba cadastral survey (kaqa-dominant Aymara). UR037's kaqa x2 walled compounds follow the Aymara cadastral vocabulary documented at UR016's larger scale.

Leymebamba 1452 lunar catalog. Shares the physical-marker convention -- UR005 has red-thread markers at specific cord positions, UR037 has yellow-camelid markers. Same class of manual-retrieval annotation.

word_reading.note: 21 STRING at 100 percent dict hit (post chi->ki correction). kiki, chiqa, ki, kaqa, taqa, paqa all dictionary-stable. The chi->ki onset correction is principled and corpus-validated.

document_type.note: census_register robustly supported by (a) kiki x11 individual-dominance, (b) kaqa zone-segmentation, (c) paradigmatic 3-case header, (d) chiqa verification closing, (e) colour-value joint encoding. Clearest census exemplar in the corpus.

narrative.note: The 'two-zone walled-compound census' reading is internally coherent and directly supported by the kaqa structural anchors + kiki irregular entries + chiqa verification + OKR physical markers. The corpus's most legible census-register narrative.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent dict hit post-correction. Physical annotations (beginning-knot tri-colour threads + yellow camelid markers C165-166) OKR-preserved. 12 percent STRING density is normal for a census register. No Ascher layer summarised in the deep-read.

sections.title: Paradigmatic Declaration + Totals
sections.reading: 3 paired L1+L2 cords carry the word ki in 3 grammatical cases (ki-PASS, ki-DIR, TOP-ki) paired with the totals 42 and 24. The opening 'title page' declares the census subject: 'Concerning the counted selves, 42 + 24.' Beginning-knot carries tri-colour threads (light brown + light blue + red) per OKR.

sections.title: First Zone -- 14 Sub-Sections of Pairs
sections.reading: C7 kaqa = opens the FIRST WALLED COMPOUND. 14 sub-sections follow, each counting in pairs (value=2 dominant). Colour-value encoding fires: AB=2 (pairs), BG=1 (individuals), MB=4 (subtotals). The zone's population is counted pair by pair.

sections.title: Second Zone -- Individual Registrations
sections.reading: C81 kaqa = opens the SECOND WALLED COMPOUND. 29 persons counted in this zone. kiki entries now appear with regularity: C90, C102, C110, C112, C113, C119, C122. Seven individual selves registered within the paired structure -- exceptions to the default pair-count.

sections.title: Geographic Closing
sections.reading: C128 taqa (administrative boundary) + C131 paqa (east / dawn orientation) + C133 chiqa (verification). The surveyed area's geographic frame is established at the zone-transition point.

sections.title: Final Register + Camelid Markers
sections.reading: Sparse kiki entries continue. At C165-C166, YELLOW CAMELID markers are tied -- physical flags for manual retrieval (OKR-attested). Last cord C171 BG chiqa = FINAL VERIFICATION. The census closes on truth.

key_cords.note: LAST CORD = FINAL VERIFICATION on beige-gray

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Cadastral census analysis -- chi->ki onset correction convention.
Murra, J. V. (1980). The Economic Organization of the Inka State.
Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots -- camelid-wool marker conventions.
Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara -- kaqa (wall).
Amano, Y. (1961). Chu-oh Andesu bunmei. Museo Amano collection catalog.
Museo Amano, Lima -- UR037 catalogue record R4248.
OKR cord-note on UR037: beginning-knot coloured threads (light brown, light blue, red); yellow camelid markers on cords 165-166.

Compare UR198 (Nazca census), AS026B (lineage register), UR177 (Chuquitanta workshop), UR050 (paradigmatic text), HP051 (compact identity register), UR016 (Leymebamba cadastral), UR005 (red-thread marker convention).
